title: Unified Error Envelope with Request ID Propagation Pattern
summary: A standardized error handling pattern for HTTP APIs: every response (success or error) includes a request_id for log correlation. Error responses use a unified envelope with code (machine-readable), message (human-readable), and request_id fields. Business exceptions and system exceptions are handled separately — business errors return structured codes, system errors are caught by a global exception handler that logs the full traceback but returns a sanitized envelope. Sensitive fields (Authorization, Cookie, API key, database password, private key) are stripped from logs. Critical write operations record audit log entries with request_id.
why_reusable: Every API service needs error handling. This pattern defines the minimum viable error infrastructure: unified envelope shape, request ID propagation, sanitized logging, and audit trail — applicable to FastAPI, Express, NestJS, Flask, or any HTTP framework.
adapter_target: Any backend service — implement as middleware (FastAPI) or interceptor (Express/NestJS) that wraps all endpoints.
implementation_notes: Error envelope shape: { error: { code, message, request_id, timestamp } }
Request ID: generate at entry point (UUID4), attach to request context, include in all logs and responses
Global exception handler: catch unhandled exceptions → log full traceback with request_id → return sanitized envelope
Business errors: raise typed exceptions (NotFoundError, ValidationError) that map to HTTP status codes and error codes
Log sanitization: strip Authorization header, Cookie header, API keys, passwords, private keys before writing
Audit log: record who, what, when, request_id for all write operations (POST/PUT/DELETE)
tests_required: Unit test: error envelope contains all required fields
Unit test: business error maps to correct HTTP status and error code
Unit test: global handler returns sanitized envelope, not stack trace
Unit test: log sanitization removes Authorization header
Integration test: request_id is consistent across logs and error response
Integration test: audit log records write operations with request_id
risks: Request ID middleware must be the first middleware in the chain — if it fails, no correlation is possible
Sanitization is pattern-based and may miss novel secret formats
Audit logging adds latency to write operations; use async logging or a queue for high-throughput services
Error code taxonomy must be stable — changing codes breaks client error handling
do_not_do: Do not return stack traces or internal error details to clients
Do not log Authorization headers, cookies, API keys, or passwords
Do not use different error shapes for different endpoints
Do not swallow exceptions and return HTTP 200 with an error field in the body
notes: Derived from backend domain rules/error-handling-and-logging-rules.md. The pattern is framework-agnostic — describes the interface shape and conventions, not specific implementations.
